meta.title: OnPush order row stays stale after a live update
meta.summary: A live order update arrives, but the row keeps showing the old status until the user clicks, sorts, or filters the table.
signals: The socket event arrives, but the row still shows the old status
Sorting or filtering suddenly makes the right status appear
The service mutates the existing order object in place

context.symptom: Support sees an order move from Pending to Shipped in the event log, but the status badge in that row stays old until someone clicks sort, changes a filter, or reopens the page.
context.userImpact: The dashboard feels unreliable, so agents refresh manually or double-check orders in another tool.
context.environment: Angular 17 admin table. Each row is an `OnPush` child component that receives `@Input() order`, and live updates patch the shared orders array when a socket event arrives.

evidence.title: What users noticed
evidence.body: If someone clicks Sort by Updated or toggles a filter, the row suddenly shows the correct status without another event arriving.

evidence.title: Live event log
evidence.body: The socket payload for order #1842 arrives on time, and the service logs `status: "shipped"`, but the row still shows `Pending`.

evidence.title: Current update path

stages.title: Stage 1: likely root cause
stages.prompt: Why does the row stay stale?
stages.helperText: Pick the explanation that matches both `OnPush` and the fact that another interaction suddenly fixes the UI.

options.label: The socket library must be completely outside Angular, so change detection never runs anywhere in the page.
options.feedback: That can happen in some apps, but it does not fit the clue that a normal Angular interaction instantly reveals the already-updated value.

options.label: The live update mutates the same order object, so the `OnPush` row does not get a meaningful input change until another trigger happens.
options.feedback: Correct. The row uses `OnPush`, but the update keeps the same object reference, so the child can stay visually stale until another check happens.

options.label: The table probably uses `trackBy`, and that keeps Angular from ever repainting the row content.
options.feedback: `trackBy` can help reuse DOM nodes, but it is not the main reason an `OnPush` child stays stale after an in-place mutation.

options.label: Angular templates cannot reliably react to nested object field updates, even when the data is correct.
options.feedback: Angular can render nested fields. The issue here is the trigger model around `OnPush` and object identity.

stages.title: Stage 2: debug priority order
stages.prompt: What would you check first? Put the best three at the top.
stages.helperText: First prove that the event arrives, then prove whether the update changes data identity or only mutates existing state.

candidates.label: Confirm the live event arrives and the target order object really changes in the service.
candidates.description: Start with the incoming patch and the exact object being updated.

candidates.label: Compare the order and orders-array references before and after the update.
candidates.description: This tells you whether Angular sees a meaningful identity change.

candidates.label: Try one immutable replacement or `markForCheck()` path to see whether the stale row updates immediately.
candidates.description: A small repro confirms the trigger model before a larger refactor.

candidates.label: Remove `trackBy` from the table before measuring anything else.
candidates.description: Possible red herring, weak first move.

candidates.label: Change the whole feature back to Default change detection first.
candidates.description: Too broad before you prove the real boundary problem.

stages.title: Stage 3: best fix set
stages.prompt: Which changes belong in the real fix?
stages.helperText: Pick the changes that repair the update boundary instead of hiding the symptom.

options.label: Remove `OnPush` from every row and parent component so the table always gets checked.
options.feedback: That can mask the symptom, but it throws away a useful performance boundary instead of fixing the stale-update path.

options.label: Replace the updated order with a new object and publish a new list reference instead of mutating in place.
options.feedback: Correct. This gives `OnPush` a real identity change to work with.

options.label: Wrap the mutation in `setTimeout` so Angular gets another chance to notice it later.
options.feedback: That may move timing around, but it is not a clean fix for stale identity.

options.label: Keep live row state in a reactive stream or signal boundary that updates with new references instead of mutating service fields in place.
options.feedback: Correct. Reactive state boundaries work well with `OnPush` when updates are explicit and immutable.

options.label: If one mutable boundary must stay, trigger `markForCheck()` there instead of hoping a later click will repaint the row.
options.feedback: Correct. This is a reasonable fallback when you cannot make the whole path immutable immediately.

stages.title: Stage 4: regression guard
stages.prompt: What is the best guardrail for this bug?
stages.helperText: Pick the guard that checks whether a live update repaints the row without a rescue click.

options.label: Monitor socket connection health and event delivery time, because the real problem was delayed live data.
options.feedback: Connection health matters, but it does not catch the UI staying stale after the event already arrives.

options.label: Add a component or integration test that pushes a live update and asserts the row text changes without any extra user interaction.
options.feedback: Correct. This checks the exact regression: event arrives, UI should repaint immediately.

options.label: Have QA sort the table once before release and verify the new status appears somewhere.
options.feedback: That can miss the real bug because sorting itself is one of the actions that hides it.

options.label: Avoid `OnPush` on any row that might ever receive a live update.
options.feedback: That is a blanket restriction, not a guardrail. The goal is to test the update path, not ban the strategy.

scoreBands.label: You saw the stale UI, but not the identity problem
scoreBands.summary: The live data path was active, but the real issue was still the `OnPush` boundary not seeing a meaningful change.

scoreBands.label: Good Angular instincts, but the fix path was still mixed
scoreBands.summary: You connected the bug to change detection, but one part of the answer still leaned on broad workarounds.

scoreBands.label: You diagnosed the `OnPush` boundary clearly
scoreBands.summary: You proved the event path, found the in-place mutation, and chose a fix that makes live updates explicit again.

idealRunbook: Confirm the live event arrives and the service really applies the patch.
Check whether the update creates a new order or only mutates the old one.
Prove the trigger model with one immutable replacement or a `markForCheck()` repro.
Lock the fix with a UI test that updates a row while the view stays otherwise idle.

teachingBlocks.title: Angular shortcut
teachingBlocks.text: When an `OnPush` child looks stale, ask one question early: did we create a new reference, or did we only mutate the old object?

teachingBlocks.title: Strong answer signals
items: You separate event delivery from UI repainting.
You talk about object identity, not just 'change detection is weird'.
You prefer immutable updates or a clear reactive boundary over timing hacks.

debrief.optionalReflectionPrompt: If the team insisted on mutating in place, where would you put `markForCheck()` and why?
